mission.title: Install and Configure k0s (Zero Friction Kubernetes)
mission.description: k0s is a lightweight, all-inclusive Kubernetes distribution designed for ease of use and minimal friction in deployment. It is ideal for cloud, bare metal, edge, and IoT environments, allowing users to bootstrap Kubernetes clusters quickly.

steps.title: Configure kubectl access
steps.description:
To access the cluster with kubectl, copy the kubeconfig file:
```bash
sudo cp /var/lib/k0s/pki/admin.conf ~/.kube/config
``` 
Verify kubectl access:
```bash
kubectl get nodes
```
This should list the nodes in your cluster.

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Enable the metrics server for monitoring:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
```
Verify the metrics server is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
```

uninstall.title: Remove k0s cluster
uninstall.description:
Stop and uninstall the k0s controller and clean up resources:
```bash
sudo k0s stop
sudo k0s uninstall
rm -rf /var/lib/k0s
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade k0s to v1.31
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up your cluster configuration:
```bash
sudo k0s backup
```
To upgrade to v1.31, run:
```bash
curl -sSL https://get.k0s.sh | sudo sh
```
After the upgrade, restart the k0s controller:
```bash
sudo k0s restart
```
Verify the upgrade:
```bash
k0s version
```

troubleshooting.title: Cluster not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the cluster fails to start, check the logs:
```bash
sudo journalctl -u k0s-controller
```
Look for errors related to configuration or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl access issues
troubleshooting.description:
If kubectl commands fail, ensure the kubeconfig file is correctly set:
```bash
echo $KUBECONFIG
```
If it’s empty, set it to the correct path:
```bash
export KUBECONFIG=/var/lib/k0s/pki/admin.conf
```

troubleshooting.title: Metrics server not available
troubleshooting.description:
If the metrics server is not available, check its pod status:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
```
If it's not running, check the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs -n kube-system <metrics-server-pod-name>
```

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance and stability, added support for new Kubernetes features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.29.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced enhanced security features and better integration with cloud providers.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of k0s will have a running Kubernetes cluster with all nodes in a ready state, and kubectl will be able to communicate with the cluster. You should also have monitoring enabled and be able to view node metrics.
codeSnippets: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Linux environment with internet access and necessary permissions.
